name: Consistent histories (Griffiths-Omnès-Gell-Mann-Hartle)
summary: Reformulates quantum mechanics in terms of probabilities for sequences of events ('histories'), subject to a 'consistency' (decoherence) condition that classical-style probability rules apply. Eliminates the measurement postulate by treating measurement as just one type of physical history. Compatible with unitary evolution + decoherence; gives the same predictions as standard QM but provides a logical framework for talking about closed quantum systems without observers.

addresses_puzzles.note: Provides a logical framework where probabilities over histories satisfy classical rules when the consistency condition holds. Doesn't pick a unique history; in this sense closer to many-worlds (all consistent histories happen) than to objective collapse (one history is selected). Decoherence is the mechanism that makes histories classical-like.

introduces.note: Reformulation of QM with no new physics; uses standard unitary evolution + decoherence.

history: Griffiths (1984), 'Consistent histories and the interpretation of quantum mechanics', J. Stat. Phys. 36, 219. Omnès (1988, 1992) developed logical formulation. Gell-Mann and Hartle (1990, 1993) generalised to 'decoherent histories' applicable to closed cosmological systems. Comprehensive review: Griffiths (2002), 'Consistent Quantum Theory', Cambridge UP.

references.citation: Griffiths, 'Consistent histories and the interpretation of quantum mechanics', J. Stat. Phys. 36, 219 (1984)
Gell-Mann and Hartle, 'Classical equations for quantum systems', Phys. Rev. D 47, 3345 (1993)
